past_medical_history: Migraines with aura since childhood (age 9), occurring monthly, characterized by visual scotomata followed by unilateral throbbing headache
Iron deficiency anemia, diagnosed at age 28, attributed to heavy menstrual periods
Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed at age 27, managed with SSRI
family_history: Mother had an intracranial hemorrhage at age 45 in Korea — limited details available, patient was told it was a 'brain bleed'
Father alive and healthy at age 60
No known family history of connective tissue disorders or coagulopathies

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.occupation: Software engineer at a technology startup, high-stress work environment
social_history.exercise: Attends yoga 2-3 times per week, occasional hiking

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cooperative but anxious. Speech is telegraphic with reduced fluency — uses short phrases, omits function words, but comprehension is fully preserved. Able to follow 2-step commands without difficulty. Naming moderately impaired (6/10 on confrontation naming). Repetition impaired for complex sentences. No neglect on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Right lower facial droop, central pattern (forehead sparing). No tongue deviation. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically.
neurological_exam.motor: Left upper and lower extremity 5/5 throughout. Right arm: deltoid 4/5, biceps 4/5, triceps 3/5, wrist extension 3/5, grip strength 2/5, finger abduction 2/5. Right leg: hip flexion 4+/5, knee extension 5-/5, ankle dorsiflexion 4+/5. Right pronator drift present.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally. Proprioception intact in all extremities. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left upper and lower extremity 2+ symmetric. Right upper extremity 3+. Right lower extremity 2+. Right plantar response extensor (Babinski positive). Left plantar flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Left finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal. Right-sided testing limited by weakness but no additional dysmetria beyond what weakness accounts for.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly unsteady with right leg circumduction. Able to ambulate with assistance. Right foot tends to drag.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 8. Breakdown: facial palsy 1, right arm motor 2, right leg motor 1, best language (aphasia) 2, dysarthria 1, right hand motor 1.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden right arm weakness and difficulty speaking
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Eunice Park is a 32-year-old right-handed Korean-American woman who presents to the emergency department via EMS with acute-onset right arm weakness and expressive language difficulty beginning approximately 2 hours ago. She was at her desk at work when she suddenly noticed difficulty typing with her right hand, which rapidly progressed to inability to lift her right arm. Simultaneously, she found herself unable to form complete sentences, though she understood everything being said to her. A coworker called 911. On further history, the patient reports a 6-month history of recurrent episodes of transient right-hand weakness and word-finding difficulty, each lasting 10 to 30 minutes and resolving completely. She notes that these episodes were consistently triggered by hyperventilation — occurring during intense exercise at yoga, after crying spells, and during panic attacks. Her primary care physician attributed these episodes to anxiety and panic disorder, and her escitalopram dose was increased 4 months ago. She has had approximately 8 such episodes. She also describes childhood-onset migraines with visual aura (scintillating scotomata) beginning at age 9; she was told these were 'complicated migraines.' She has never had a formal stroke evaluation. She denies recent trauma, neck manipulation, febrile illness, or recreational drug use. She takes combined oral contraceptive pills for menstrual regulation. She is particularly worried because her mother, who lived in Korea, suffered a 'brain bleed' at age 45 and required neurosurgery, but the family has limited details about the diagnosis.

findings.location: Left frontal operculum (Broca area), left MCA superior division territory

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal consistent with restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No definite FLAIR hyperintensity (consistent with acute, <6 hour infarct)
signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintensity in left frontal operculum

findings.borders: Poorly defined margins in left frontal opercular region

findings.location: Bilateral watershed zones (left > right): bilateral frontal and parietal border zone territories

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense foci consistent with chronic ischemic change

findings.borders: Well-defined, consistent with remote infarcts in watershed distribution

findings.location: Bilateral basal ganglia and thalamic regions

signal_characteristics.T2: Prominent serpentine flow voids in bilateral basal ganglia, more prominent on the left

additional_observations: Prominent bilateral flow voids in the basal ganglia region are suggestive of collateral vessel formation
MRA of intracranial circulation demonstrates diminished flow signal in bilateral distal ICA and proximal MCA segments
mri.impression: Multiple bilateral chronic watershed infarcts suggesting longstanding hemodynamic compromise. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Normal PR, QRS, and QTc intervals
No atrial enlargement or ventricular hypertrophy
ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead electrocardiogram. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes identified.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 35.4 % (L), MCV 78.2 fL (L), RDW 15.8 % (H), Ferritin 14 ng/mL (L), Iron 38 mcg/dL (L), TIBC 420 mcg/dL (H).

findings.location: Bilateral internal carotid arteries, supraclinoid segments

signal_characteristics.CTA: Severe stenosis of bilateral ICA terminal segments (supraclinoid ICA). Left ICA terminus >90% stenosed. Right ICA terminus approximately 80% stenosed. Abrupt tapering with near-occlusion bilaterally.

findings.type: Bilateral MCA stenosis with moyamoya collaterals
findings.location: Bilateral MCA M1 segments and basal skull base

signal_characteristics.CTA: Bilateral MCA M1 segments severely stenotic with minimal residual flow. Extensive network of small tortuous collateral vessels at the skull base, creating the classic 'puff of smoke' (moyamoya) appearance. Prominent lenticulostriate and thalamoperforator collaterals bilaterally.

Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with normal caliber. Basilar artery patent. Bilateral PCAs patent without stenosis. No posterior circulation involvement. Posterior communicating arteries provide some collateral flow to anterior circulation.

additional_observations: Extensive transdural collateral vessels from external carotid artery branches (middle meningeal, superficial temporal) providing supplementary flow to the anterior circulation
No aneurysm identified at this time
No evidence of dissection in cervical or intracranial vessels
output.impression: Severe bilateral ICA terminal segment stenosis with bilateral MCA M1 stenosis. Extensive moyamoya-pattern collateral network at the skull base — classic 'puff of smoke' appearance. Posterior circulation is spared. Transdural collaterals from external carotid branches providing supplementary anterior circulation flow. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.type: Moyamoya disease, Suzuki stage III bilaterally
findings.location: Bilateral ICA terminal segments, bilateral MCA and ACA origins

signal_characteristics.Angiography: Left ICA injection: severe terminal ICA stenosis with near-occlusion. Left MCA M1 is threadlike with minimal antegrade flow. Left ACA A1 is severely stenosed. Extensive basal moyamoya collateral network arising from perforating arteries — intensification of the moyamoya cloud. Right ICA injection: similar findings with severe terminal ICA stenosis. Right MCA M1 stenosed to <1mm residual lumen. Right ACA A1 moderately stenosed. Bilateral moyamoya collateral network at skull base (Suzuki stage III: intensified moyamoya vessels with ICA terminal stenosis).

Bilateral external carotid artery injections demonstrate prominent transdural collateral supply via middle meningeal arteries, superficial temporal arteries, and occipital arteries providing leptomeningeal anastomoses to cortical MCA branches. Left side has more developed transdural collaterals than right.

Bilateral vertebral artery injections demonstrate patent posterior circulation without stenosis. Posterior communicating arteries are small but present, providing limited anterior-posterior collateral flow.

additional_observations: Cerebral circulation time is prolonged bilaterally, more so on the left
output.impression: Bilateral moyamoya disease, Suzuki stage III. Severe bilateral ICA terminal and MCA/ACA proximal stenosis with extensive basal collateral network. Prominent transdural collateral supply from external carotid branches bilaterally. Posterior circulation is spared. Neurosurgical revascularization (STA-MCA bypass or encephaloduroarteriosynangiosis) should be considered, particularly given the left hemisphere acute infarct and bilateral hemodynamic compromise. Clinical correlation recommended.

Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.value: Trace mitral regurgitation, physiologic. No other valvular abnormalities.
Intact interatrial septum. Negative bubble study — no right-to-left shunt at rest or with Valsalva.

findings.location: Bilateral MCA territories, worse on the left

signal_characteristics.CBF: Reduced bilateral cerebral blood flow, left hemisphere 28 mL/100g/min (normal >40), right hemisphere 32 mL/100g/min
signal_characteristics.CBV: Relatively preserved in most territories due to autoregulatory compensation, mildly reduced in left MCA territory
signal_characteristics.MTT: Prolonged bilaterally, left > right. Left MCA territory MTT 8.2 seconds (normal <4), right MCA territory 6.8 seconds
signal_characteristics.Tmax: Elevated bilaterally. Left hemisphere Tmax >6 seconds involving approximately 45% of left MCA territory. Right hemisphere Tmax >6 seconds involving approximately 30% of right MCA territory

signal_characteristics.Post_acetazolamide: Minimal augmentation of CBF bilaterally after acetazolamide (Diamox) administration. Left hemisphere CBF increased only to 30 mL/100g/min (normal >50% increase). Right hemisphere increased to 36 mL/100g/min. Cerebral vascular reserve is severely exhausted bilaterally.
signal_characteristics.Steal_phenomenon: Paradoxical decrease in CBF in the left frontal watershed territory after acetazolamide — consistent with vascular steal phenomenon where vasodilation in normal territories diverts flow away from maximally vasodilated, stenotic territories

additional_observations: Posterior circulation perfusion is preserved (PCA territories normal CBF bilaterally)
The extensive bilateral perfusion deficit with exhausted cerebrovascular reserve explains the patient's vulnerability to ischemic events during hyperventilation
Steal phenomenon confirms that the hemispheric blood flow is maximally compensated and has no remaining vasodilatory reserve
output.impression: Severe bilateral hemispheric hypoperfusion in MCA territories (left > right), with profoundly impaired cerebrovascular reserve on acetazolamide challenge. Vascular steal phenomenon in left frontal watershed territory. The impaired reserve explains the patient's vulnerability to TIAs during hyperventilation (which reduces pCO2 and causes cerebral vasoconstriction in normal territories, stealing flow from already maximally dilated moyamoya-dependent territories). Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke secondary to moyamoya disease

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal echocardiogram with negative bubble study excludes PFO; bilateral vessel disease and watershed infarct pattern not explained by cardioembolism
Patient is 32 with no vascular risk factors; bilateral ICA terminal stenosis with moyamoya collaterals in a young East Asian woman is classic moyamoya, not atherosclerotic disease
Bilateral symmetric ICA terminal stenosis with moyamoya collaterals is distinct from the multifocal irregular stenoses of vasculitis; normal inflammatory markers (ESR 8, CRP 0.3); ANA negative
Stenosis is intracranial at ICA terminus bilaterally, not cervical; no dissection flap, mural hematoma, or string sign in cervical segments on CTA

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and perform rapid neurological assessment including NIHSS scoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 8 with expressive aphasia and right hemiparesis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI and MRA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left frontal opercular infarct, bilateral chronic watershed infarcts, prominent basal ganglia flow voids, diminished bilateral ICA/MCA flow

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and inflammatory markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild iron deficiency anemia; normal coagulation and inflammatory markers; negative ANA and lupus anticoagulant

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA or MRA of head and neck to characterize the bilateral ICA stenosis and collateral pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral ICA terminal stenosis with moyamoya collaterals — diagnostic of moyamoya disease

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue combined oral contraceptive pills (estrogen increases stroke risk in moyamoya)
Avoid IV tPA given chronic bilateral stenosis and collateral-dependent circulation
Neurosurgical consultation for revascularization (STA-MCA bypass or EDAS)
Order perfusion imaging with acetazolamide challenge to assess cerebrovascular reserve
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hypoperfusion with exhausted cerebrovascular reserve and steal phenomenon

optimal_actions.action: Screen family members for moyamoya given mother's history of intracranial hemorrhage at 45
Educate patient to avoid hyperventilation, dehydration, and hypotension — all of which worsen cerebral ischemia in moyamoya

critical_actions: Obtain cerebrovascular imaging (CTA/MRA) showing bilateral ICA terminal stenosis with moyamoya collaterals
Avoid IV tPA given chronic stenosis and collateral-dependent circulation
Neurosurgical consultation for revascularization (STA-MCA bypass or encephaloduroarteriosynangiosis)
Screen for moyamoya in family members given mother's hemorrhagic stroke history
Avoid hypotension and hyperventilation which worsen cerebral ischemia in moyamoya
contraindicated_actions: IV thrombolysis without understanding the vascular anatomy — risk of hemorrhage in collateral-dependent tissue
Aggressive blood pressure lowering (collateral-dependent circulation requires maintained perfusion pressure)
Prescribing vasoconstrictors (triptans for migraines) which reduce collateral blood flow
Catheter angiography without being prepared for possible bypass surgery planning
key_reasoning_points: Recurrent stereotyped hemispheric TIAs triggered by hyperventilation is pathognomonic for moyamoya disease — hyperventilation reduces pCO2, causing cerebral vasoconstriction in normal territories while moyamoya-dependent territories are already maximally vasodilated, resulting in flow failure (steal phenomenon)
Bilateral ICA terminal stenosis with moyamoya collaterals ('puff of smoke' vessels) in a young East Asian woman is the classic presentation of moyamoya disease

Mother's intracranial hemorrhage at age 45 in Korea strongly suggests familial moyamoya — Japanese/Korean ethnicity has the highest worldwide prevalence, and hemorrhagic stroke is a common moyamoya presentation in adults
Multiple bilateral watershed infarcts on MRI indicate chronic hemodynamic compromise rather than embolic events — watershed zones are the first to suffer in low-flow states
Normal cardiac workup (echo with negative bubble study) and entirely negative hypercoagulability panel exclude the most common young stroke etiologies (PFO, dissection, thrombophilia)
Childhood migraines with aura may have been early manifestations of moyamoya-related cerebral hypoperfusion misdiagnosed as primary migraine

red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests hormonal estrogen exposure as the sole explanation for stroke in a young woman
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: OCPs increase stroke risk and must be discontinued, but they do not explain the bilateral ICA terminal stenosis or moyamoya collateral network — they are a contributing risk factor, not the primary etiology

red_herrings.data_point: Anxiety disorder diagnosis with prior TIAs attributed to panic attacks
red_herrings.intended_effect: Leads clinician to dismiss recurrent neurological episodes as psychiatric
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Recurrent stereotyped hemispheric symptoms (arm weakness, aphasia) triggered specifically by hyperventilation should raise moyamoya suspicion; anxiety-related hyperventilation is the trigger, not the cause

red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests migraine-related stroke (migrainous infarction) as primary etiology
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: These childhood episodes may represent early moyamoya-related ischemic events misdiagnosed as migraine with aura — moyamoya is a progressive disease that often begins in childhood

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle — 32-year-old Korean-American woman with recurrent TIAs dismissed as panic attacks, now presenting with acute left MCA stroke. Initial workup focuses on common young-stroke etiologies (PFO, dissection, hypercoagulability), all of which are negative. The key is recognizing bilateral ICA terminal stenosis with moyamoya collaterals on vascular imaging, triggered by the MRI finding of bilateral watershed chronic infarcts and basal ganglia flow voids. Family history of ICH in Korean mother is a critical clue.
clinical_pearls: Moyamoya disease has the highest prevalence in East Asian populations (Japan, Korea, China) with an incidence approximately 10x higher than in Western populations
Hyperventilation-triggered TIAs are nearly pathognomonic for moyamoya — the mechanism is pCO2-mediated steal phenomenon from already maximally vasodilated territories
Moyamoya can present as ischemic stroke (more common in children) or hemorrhagic stroke (more common in adults) — the mother's ICH at 45 is consistent with familial moyamoya
The RNF213 gene variant p.R4810K is the strongest genetic risk factor for moyamoya in East Asians and should be tested in confirmed cases
Bilateral watershed infarcts on MRI are a hallmark of hemodynamic stroke and should prompt vascular imaging to evaluate for proximal stenosis, especially moyamoya in East Asian patients
